title: Universal Magnetic Structure Prediction from Atomic Coordinates with Near-Experimental Accuracy
authors: Abhijatmedhi Chotrattanapituk, Ryotaro Okabe, Eunbi Rha, Mariya Al-Hinai, Eugene Jiang, Daniel Pajerowski, Yongqiang Cheng, Joshua J. Turner, Mingda Li
abstract: Magnetic order is a fundamental property of materials, governing collective behavior and enabling a broad range of functionalities. Yet magnetic structure remains difficult to determine: experiments are costly and specialized, while first-principles methods often struggle with the noncollinear and incommensurate orders found in real materials. Here we introduce magnetic structure network (MSN), an E(3) equivariant graph neural network that predicts both collinear and non-collinear magnetic structures directly from atomic crystal structures, trained directly on experimentally determined structures from MAGNDATA. By proposing the primitive modulated structure representation (PMSR), we are able to encode commensurate and incommensurate structures in a unified way without symmetry assumptions. The model achieves strong performance across all modulation components and reconstructs experimental magnetic structures with high fidelity. Our approach provides a scalable framework for rapid magnetic structure prediction and opens a route to data-driven discovery of magnetic materials.
summary: This paper presents the Magnetic Structure Network (MSN), an E(3)-equivariant GNN designed to predict complex magnetic structures directly from atomic coordinates. It overcomes limitations in current methods by using the Primitive Modulated Structure Representation (PMSR) to unify the description of both simple and incommensurate magnetic orders. This provides a powerful, data-driven framework for accelerating the discovery of magnetic materials.
main_problem: Determining the magnetic structure of materials is challenging due to costly experiments and the difficulty first-principles methods have with noncollinear or incommensurate magnetic orders.
main_result: The proposed Magnetic Structure Network (MSN) successfully predicts full magnetic structures directly from atomic coordinates with high fidelity across commensurate and incommensurate phases.
method: The authors introduce the E(3)-equivariant Graph Neural Network (MSN) trained on experimental data, utilizing the Primitive Modulated Structure Representation (PMSR) to unify the description of all magnetic periodicities.
model_or_system: The model targets magnetic crystalline materials, predicting magnetic order parameters (Fourier components and propagation vectors) from the underlying atomic crystal structure.
key_observables: Magnetic structure (collinear/non-collinear), propagation vectors (k), and site-specific magnetic moment Fourier components.
important_parameters: Training data size (MAGNDATA, >2,300 structures); the cutoff radius for graph construction.
main_assumptions: The model assumes a Heisenberg-type description for magnetic interactions, neglecting explicit anisotropic spin interactions; it also handles mathematical ambiguities related to global phase/rotation freedom.
figures_summary:
Figures illustrate the PMSR's ability to unify representations, the MSN's architecture (graph construction, E(3) message passing), and comparative predictions for $k=0$, rational $k
eq 0$, and irrational incommensurate $k$.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the limitations of existing methods, then introduces the PMSR to standardize representation, details the E(3)-equivariant MSN architecture, and finally presents the training objectives and reconstruction process.
why_interesting: While focused on magnetism, the use of E(3)-equivariant GNNs and advanced representation learning (PMSR) for complex physical states is highly relevant to developing ML tools for quantum materials simulation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Primitive Modulated Structure Representation (PMSR). Commensurate structures both with (middle row) and without (top row) non-zero propagation vectors (k) can be represented as periodic structures called magnetic unit cells that are rational multiples of their chemical unit cells. In contrast, by definition, there is no number of chemical unit cell that can capture the periodicity of incommensurate structures (bottom row). The modulated structure representation can captures the essences of all magnetic structure classes by, on top of embedding atomic sites in a unit cell with the magnetic moment vectors (m), providing how the magnetic moment vectors change (get modulated) when one...

FIG. 2. Magnetic structure network (MSN). a) Construction of periodic graph of each material structure with atoms in a primitive chemical unit cell as nodes and edges defined only between neighboring atoms with interatomic distance under certain cut off radius rmax. b) E(3)-equivariant message passing with connections according to graph in a. c) Outputs of the model which can be divided into node level and graph level outputs which require extra aggregation from all nodes at the last layer of the model. The prediction of magnetic propagation vectors which are the properties of the whole structure uses the graph level output while the prediction of site-specific Fourier components uses the...

FIG. 3. Predicted magnetic structures. For each material (MAGNDATA ID, formula), the ground-truth structure from MAGNDATA (top) is shown alongside the model prediction (bottom). (a) Commensurate structures with zero propagation vector (k = 0). (b) Commensurate structures with a non-zero rational propagation vector. The visualization supercell is constructed from the ground-truth k-vector (expanding up to a factor of 2 per lattice direction) to make the spatial modulation of magnetic moments visible. (c) Incommensurate structures: the propagation vector has an irrational component, and the same supercell convention is applied for visualization. Moment directions are shown as arrows on each...
